coredata.dc:title: Multifunctionalgraphenefilled silicone encapsulant for high-performance light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Jie
Bao, Feng
Wu, Qiangxian
Ma, Rui
Han, Xiaobing
Jin, Danping
Chen, Kaiyue
He, Junying
Guo, Zhongfa
Yan, Chunjie
coredata.dc:description: Graphene is well-dispersed into a silicone encapsulant of a light-emitting diode (LED) through a simple solvent-exchange method. The embedded graphene sheets play a multifunctional role in producing a high-performance encapsulant. Compared with pure silicone encapsulant, the tensile strength of the graphene-based materials significantly increased by 46% and elongation at break decreased only by 7% with the incorporation of 0.1wt% graphene. Furthermore, the presence of graphene can absorb twice as much UV light as pure silicone, which can protect chip and encapsulant. High transmittance values (>90%) were achieved over 400–800nm for 5mm-thick encapsulant film containing 0.01wt% graphene. The refractive index, thermal stability, and dimensional stability also dramatically increased. Owing to their multifunctionality, these hybrid materials are feasible candidates for LED encapsulation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Silicone resin
Encapsulant
Property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene-filled silicone encapsulant composites
Curing procedure of graphene-filled silicone encapsulant

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photographs of pure and graphene filled silicone encapsulant. (b) Illustration of the function of graphene in the silicone encapsulant.
(a) TEM image of GO. (b) XPS spectra of GO and graphene.
SEM images of silicone encapsulant with various concentration of graphene.
Mechanical properties of silicone encapsulant with various concentration of graphene.
UV spectra of silicone encapsulant with various concentration of graphene.
Visible light transmittance spectra of silicone encapsulant with various concentration of graphene.
Refractive index of silicone encapsulant with various concentration of graphene.
TGA curves of silicone encapsulant with various concentration of graphene.
Thermal expansion coefficient of silicone encapsulant with various concentration of graphene.

head.ce:title: Multifunctionalgraphenefilled silicone encapsulant for high-performance light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Central China Normal University, Wuhan 430079, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Nano-Geomaterials of Ministry of Education, China University of Geosciences, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Nano-Geomaterials of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene is well-dispersed into a silicone encapsulant of a light-emitting diode (LED) through a simple solvent-exchange method. The embedded graphene sheets play a multifunctional role in producing a high-performance encapsulant. Compared with pure silicone encapsulant, the tensile strength of the graphene-based materials significantly increased by 46% and elongation at break decreased only by 7% with the incorporation of 0.1wt% graphene. Furthermore, the presence of graphene can absorb twice as much UV light as pure silicone, which can protect chip and encapsulant. High transmittance values (>90%) were achieved over 400–800nm for 5mm-thick encapsulant film containing 0.01wt% graphene. The refractive index, thermal stability, and dimensional stability also dramatically increased. Owing to their multifunctionality, these hybrid materials are feasible candidates for LED encapsulation.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, which consists of atomic-thick sheets of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a hexagonal arrangement, is receiving considerable attention from researchers for the fabrication of polymer nanocomposites [1–4]. Its Yang’s modulus (∼1TPa), optical transparency (∼97.7%), thermal conductivity (3000–5000 Wm−1K−1), specific surface area (2630m2 g−1), and carrier mobility (∼10000cm2V−1S−1) render graphene a good candidate for filler [5–10] and other applications, such as in field-effect transistors, photovoltaic devices, light-emitting diodes (LEDs), and biosensors [11–18].

Great attention has been paid to the solid-state lighting because the development of InGaN-based LED has excellent properties, including environmental friendliness, long life-time, energy saving, and fast response time [19,20]. The encapsulant is a capping material that protects the individual diodes. Thus, the key properties of LED encapsulant materials include excellent transparency, high refractive index, high thermal stability, high mechanical property, and chemical resistance. Many materials have been evaluated as an encapsulant, such as epoxy, polyurethane, and silicone. Thermal resistant UV-curable epoxy-siloxane hybrid materials have been synthesized for LED encapsulant in Bae’s group [21]. Silicone-based materials with high thermal stability have been recently developed and used as LED encapsulants instead of conventional epoxy LED encapsulants. Bae’s group has also synthesized thermally stable transparent sol-gel based siloxane hybrid material with high refractive index for LED encapsulation [22].

Given its excellent photoelectric properties, graphene has been used as transparent electrodes [23,24], hole transport layer [25], and quantum dots [9,26,27] in organic LED. Although much attention has been paid to graphene-based LED devices, few reports about the application of graphene in encapsulant materials exists [28]. As such, high-performance graphene embedded silicone encapsulant was fabricated through an easy solvent-exchange method. Graphene is well-dispersed into the silicone encapsulant, and its comprehensive properties were improved.

Graphite powder, concentrated sulfuric acid, sodium nitrate, potassium permanganate, hydrogen peroxide, and hydrazine hydrate were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Silicone encapsulant (RF-1458, base resin to hardener is 1:1) was obtained from Wuhan Xinruifu Science & Technology Co., Ltd. All other chemicals were analytic grade, and redistilled water was used throughout the experiment.

Graphene was prepared from the reduction of graphene oxide (GO), which was obtained from a modified Hummers method [17,18]. The detailed synthesis procedure of graphene was described in our previous work [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene-filled silicone encapsulant composites
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene-filled silicone encapsulant can be fabricated using an easy and effective solvent-exchange method [28]. Graphene was initially exfoliated in ethanol under sonication and then mixed with base silicone resin. The weight ratios of graphene and base resin were 0%, 0.02%, 0.1%, and 0.2%. The mixed solution was centrifuged and heated to remove ethanol completely, followed by stirring for 1h at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Curing procedure of graphene-filled silicone encapsulant
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, hardener was added to the filled silicone resin, and the mixture was stirred for 0.5h. Secondly, vacuum was used to remove bubbles in the mixture before thermocuring. Finally, the mixture was cured at 150°C for 3h in a Teflon mold [21,22]. Fig. 1 a shows encapsulant films with various concentrations of graphene, which revealed that graphene were readily dispersed without any aggregation.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a JEOL2100 instrument. X-ray spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD with Al Kα radiation (1486.6eV). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was conducted with a Cambridge S-250MK3 UK microscopy with an acceleration voltage of 25kV. Static mechanical property tests were conducted with the dynamical mechanical analyzer (Gaoping, Shenzhen) at room temperature with humidity about 50%, and a speed of 5mm/min. UV–vis spectra were recorded by a S-3100 spectrophotometer (Scinco, Korea). Refractive index of liquids was recorded on WAY Abbe refractometer at 25°C. The thermal stability was determined through thermo-gravimetric (TG) analysis (STA409PC, Netzsch, Germany). The height, width, and length of encapsulant films at different temperatures were determined with a Vernier caliper (Mitutoyo; minimum scale=0.01mm), and thermal expansion coefficient was calculated by the percentage change of volume as temperature change.

The fundamental role of an encapsulant is to ensure long-term durability and allow high light transmittance. Fig. 1a shows that the pure encapsulant film was transparent and colorless. The encapsulant films became gray after the introduction of graphene and finally became black when the concentration of graphene reached 0.1wt%. Multifunctional graphene was introduced to improve the mechanical properties, UV-shielding property, refractive index, and thermal stability of silicone encapsulant (Fig. 1b) [28].

SEM image is a convenient and powerful tool to probe the morphology and layer number of GO. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the GO sheets obtained by directed sonication in water, showed a wrinkled silk shape that was a typical characteristic of single GO sheet [3,4.11]. The reduction degree of GO significantly influenced the performance of composites. X-ray spectra was used to confirm the high degree of reduction. Fig. 2b shows that the C 1s spectrum of GO consisted of four different peaks: CC in aromatic rings (284.6eV), CO (286.3eV), CO (287.8eV), and COOH (289.0eV). In the XPS spectrum of graphene, the intensities of all O 1s decreased, revealing that considerable oxygen containing functional groups were removed [13,16].

The properties of polymer composites are well known to strongly rely on the dispersion state of the filler materials. However, obtaining well-dispersed graphene in the polymer matrix is challenging because of the strong π–π stacking between graphene layers [3,4]. Recently, the stable dispersion of polymer-coated graphene composites with enhanced physicochemical properties has been achieved through a solvent-exchange process [28]. To determine the quality of graphene dispersion in silicone, SEM was used in the present work. Fig. 3 shows SEM images of encapsulant films with various graphene concentrations. Based on the images of films surface (Fig. 3a–d), no aggregated graphene sheets were observed. The slight difference observed among them can be explained by increased graphene concentration, which roughened the film surface [13,18]. Thus, the graphene sheets were highly dispersed in encapsulant, which ensured that the unique properties of graphene were retained in the encapsulant [29].

Many kinds of siloxane modified graphene hybrid materials were synthesized, but few studies about polysiloxane/graphene films have been reported [30]. Graphene-filled silicone encapsulant films were successfully fabricated in the present study. To better understand the role of graphene as a reinforcing material, the mechanical properties of silicone encapsulant films with various concentrations of graphene were investigated (Fig. 4 ). The tensile strength increased with increased graphene concentration. For 0.1wt% graphene-filled encapsulant, the tensile strength is as high as 3.54MPa (increased by 46%), with elongation at break only decreased by 7%. The tensile strength is superior to vinyl, and epoxy group contained siloxane for addition-cure silicone encapsulant, which is synthesized for adhesion enhancement [31]. In addition, compared with polyurethane/graphene composites fabricated by dispersion blending, the ductility of graphene-filled silicone encapsulant was maintained at high level [32]. The improvements are attributed to the good dispersion of graphene and good interfacial adhesion between graphene and the silicone encapsulant, which is beneficial for the effective protection of LEDs [13].

Fig. 5 shows the UV absorbance spectra containing various concentrations of graphene sheets in the silicone encapsulant matrix. The UV absorbance increased as the graphene content increased in polymer matrix. For all the composites, the spectra show broad absorption in the UV region. The absorbance intensity of the composites is twice as much as that of pure silicone, with the incorporation of 0.1wt% graphene. According to the Lambert-Beer’s law, the higher the light absorbance, the lower the transmittance [33]. The absorbance of neat silicon is as low as 0.116 at 325nm, which corresponds to a transmittance of 76.5%. As the content of graphene reached 0.1wt%, the absorbance at 325nm of the composite reached as high as 2.76, and the corresponding transmittance dramatically decreased to 0.17%. The result can be ascribed to the conjugated structure of graphene [13,34,35]. This phenomenon can also be found in polyvinyl acetate filled with calcium carbonate and calcium sulfate nanoparticles, which the UV absorbance intensity increased with increased filling content [36]. As mentioned above, the UV-shielding property of filled encapsulant can be controlled by the addition of graphene. These optical properties can protect the chip and encapsulant materials from UV photodegradation, which can ensure long-term durability of LED.

High transmittance is one of the most important properties for encapsulants, which has a direct effect on the package efficiency and the reliability of the final product. Highly transparent encapsulant can improve light output, lumen maintenance, and the optical transparency for LED encapsulant materials should not be lower than 80% at 25°C [37]. Silicone encapsulant with different graphene concentration are highly transparent in visible regions (Fig. 6 ). High transmittance values (>90%) can be achieved over 400–800nm for 5mm-thick encapsulant film containing 0.01wt% graphene. Compared with neat silicone encapsulant, the transmittance of graphene modified silicone encapsulant only decreased by 2%–3%, which is consistent with the absorbance of single-layer graphene (2.3%) [38,39]. The result revealed that the graphene sheets were highly dispersed in encapsulant again. The transmittance values decrease by 4%–5% when the graphene concentration reached 0.1wt%, which was due to the increased graphene sheet in the direction of light output [28]. Although the introduction of graphene with high concentration (0.1wt%) decreased the transmittance, the obtained encapsulant also had higher transmittance than functionalized graphene laminated transparent polydimethylsiloxane membranes, which showed transmittance from ∼45% to 65% within the visible range [33]. This revealed that the introduction of graphene did not affect the transmittance of silicone encapsulant.

High refractive index of the encapsulant is another important optical property for package efficiency and product reliability, which can also enhance light output and lumen maintenance [39]. Considering that graphene has a high refractive index (2.6–3.0) [12,40,41], new high refractive index materials may be produced by filling graphene into polymer matrix. The refractive index of the silicone encapsulant with different graphene contents is shown in Fig. 7 . Pure silicone encapsulant had a low refractive index (1.406) [42], and the refractive index increased to 1.455 at a graphene weight content of 0.1wt%. Graphene greatly contributed to the increase in refractive index for silicone encapsulant. This result indicated that graphene incorporation was an effective approach to increasing the refractive index of polymer composites. This finding was consistent with that for graphene-filled poly(N,N′-dimethylacrylamine), i.e., its refractive index linearly increases from 1.516 to 1.976 with increased the weight content of graphene nanoparticles from 0wt% to 50wt% [43]. Thus, graphene can enhance the light output of silicone encapsulant.

To ensure light output, the encapsulant must be designed to be thermally stable and able to operate long term at a high junction temperature. Thermolysis can cause deterioration of light emission, decrease working lifetime, and increase power consumption. Fig. 8 illustrates the TGA curves of pure and filled silicone encapsulant. Pure silicone encapsulant sample showed excellent thermal stability, the decomposition temperature at 5% weight loss (Td,5%) exceeded 450°C. This result was much better than conventional cross-linked liquid silicone rubber designed for encapsulant [44] and close to hydroxyl-terminated copolysiloxanes containing cyanopropyl groups [45]. Thermal stability improved after the introduction of the graphene sheet. When graphene concentration reached 0.1wt%,Td,5% of the encapsulant increased by 30°C. Weight loss at 300°C decreased from 1.52% to 0.32%, as the weight content of graphene increased from 0 to 0.1wt%. The result was superior to that for GO-filled hyperbranched polysiloxane, in which the Td,5% of the composites increases by only 15°C at a GO concentration of 16.7wt% [46]. The enhanced thermal stability of the filled encapsulant was mainly attributed to the excellent thermal stability and thermal conductivity of graphene [28,43].

In addition to high thermal stability, dimensional stability upon exposure to high temperature is also important because LED operates at high junction temperature. A low coefficient of thermal expansion provides dimensional stability. As shown in Fig. 9 , the thermal expansion coefficient of pure silicone encapsulant was 84.3×10−6 K−1, which decreased to 19.6×10−6 K−1, with the addition of 0.1wt% graphene. This finding demonstrated that graphene sheets were very effective in terms of reducing the thermal expansion coefficient, because the graphene has a negative thermal expansion coefficient (−8.0×10−6 K−1) [47,48]. The reduction in thermal expansion coefficient was due to the well dispersibility of graphene in filled encapsulant, which improved dimensional stability.

Graphene was well-dispersed into silicone encapsulant using an easy solvent-exchange approach. The incorporation of 0.1wt% graphene increased the tensile strength by 46%, with the ductility remaining high. The filled silicone encapsulant exhibited good UV-shielding properties and transmittance. Meanwhile, refractive index, thermal stability, and dimensional stability remarkably increased. Therefore, the introduction of graphene can enhance the long-term stability of the encapsulant and has promising LED-encapsulation applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was support by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (CUGL100407, CUGL120118), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (41372367), the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2012 FFA111, 2010BFA022), and the public service project of the Chinese Ministry of Land and Resources (201311024).
